questions.question: What is the time complexity of Bubble Sort algorithm?
questions.explanation: Bubble Sort is a simple sorting algorithm that repeatedly steps through the list, compares adjacent elements and swaps them if they are in the wrong order. This process continues until the list is sorted.

questions.question: Which of the following sorting algorithms is an in-place sorting algorithm?
questions.explanation: Insertion Sort is an in-place sorting algorithm because it only uses a constant amount of additional space. It works by iterating through the list one element at a time, inserting each element into its proper position within the previously sorted portion of the list.

questions.question: What is the difference between Selection Sort and Insertion Sort?
options: Insertion Sort is faster than Selection Sort
Selection Sort is faster than Insertion Sort
Insertion Sort is an in-place algorithm
Selection Sort is only used for sorting objects
questions.explanation: Selection Sort selects the smallest (or largest, depending on the sort order) element from the unsorted portion of the list and swaps it with the first element of the unsorted portion. Insertion Sort, on the other hand, builds the final sorted list one element at a time by inserting each element into its proper position within the previously sorted portion of the list.

questions.question: Which sorting algorithm has a best-case time complexity of O(n log n)?
questions.explanation: Merge Sort is a divide-and-conquer algorithm that splits the list into two halves, recursively sorts each half, and then merges the two sorted halves into a single sorted list.

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of Insertion Sort?
options: Insertion Sort is faster than other algorithms
Insertion Sort is an in-place algorithm
questions.explanation: Insertion Sort is an in-place sorting algorithm because it only uses a constant amount of additional space. This makes it particularly useful for sorting large datasets in memory-constrained environments.
